_note_net_debt: Corrected from bundle's $10.3B to $5.2B per Q3 2025 balance sheet (cash $9.3B, debt $14.5B = net debt $5.2B). Post-Q1 2026 cash is likely ~$12B+ after $2.8B WBD fee receipt and Q1 FCF, so net debt may have fallen further. Using $5.2B as conservative (more debt = lower per-share value).
_note_r: Blended WACC from SOTP: Subscription annuity WACC 9.0%, Advertising growth WACC 10.5%, weighted ~70/30 on value contribution. Rounds to ~9.3% blended. Used for single-company comps reconstruction.

anchor._note: Netflix guided FCF ~$12.5B for FY2026 (Q4 2025 8-K). This is the NTM FCF anchor — the most direct cash-return measure for a subscription business. EV/FCF = ($80.50 x 4.27B + $5.2B) / $12.5B = $348.3B / $12.5B = 27.9x current.
anchor._note_roic: Netflix FY2026 NOPAT estimated at ~$12.0B (EBIT $16.1B x 75% tax retention). Invested capital is predominantly content assets + working capital (~$35-40B). ROIC = $12B / $37B = ~32-35%. Using 35% as the blended forward ROIC, consistent with high-margin subscription + emerging ad business.

street.thesis: Netflix has built the only scaled, profitable streaming platform; advertising and price hike velocity should drive revenue re-acceleration to 15%+ while margins expand toward 35%+. The 40% drawdown from the June 2025 high is a valuation opportunity, not a fundamental deterioration. FY2026 guidance maintained; FCF compounding from $9.5B to $12.5B+.

history.note: Netflix FCF CAGR 2022-2025: from $1.6B (FY2022) to $9.5B (FY2025) = approximately 82%/year absolute growth — driven by margin expansion from 17.8% to 29.5%. This is not a steady-state CAGR; it reflects operating leverage kicking in. On revenue: 16%/year CAGR FY2022-2025. The FCF CAGR is misleading as a forward guide — the right anchor is the FY2026 guided $12.5B growing toward ~$18B by FY2028.

peers.name: Spotify
peers.note: Audio streaming analog; 20% revenue growth; margin expansion story similar to Netflix 2020-2023. Higher multiple reflects faster growth rate from smaller base.

peers.name: Walt Disney (streaming segments only)
peers.note: Not a clean comp (legacy parks/linear); at 4% revenue growth it is a mature conglomerate. Netflix deserves a premium.

peers.name: Amazon (blended, for Prime Video context)
peers.note: Amazon is growing at 10% total with high-margin AWS. Netflix is not a clean Amazon comp but useful for advertising platform context.

contested_lever: Whether advertising ARPU per ad-tier subscriber scales from ~$15-20/year today toward $40-55/year by 2028-2030 — i.e., can Netflix's in-house ad tech achieve the fill rates and CPMs that justify a 15-18x EBIT multiple on the advertising segment's $6.5B in 2028 earnings contribution, or does Amazon's structural data advantage cap Netflix's ad yield and compress the platform re-rate?
